speaker: interviewer
text: Roos, in Los Angeles, California. Reel two. Mr. Roos, we left off, in the last reel, where you were describing what the synagogue was like. And you were discussing the Jewish-- the Jews who were Prussians, or the Prussian Jews. And you said, I think you were about at 10:30 in the morning, you'd know exactly where they were going to be. Can you pick up from there, do you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, one other thing about the Prussian Jews. Four brothers, their appearance were those typical Prussian Jews. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One of them was very close to me. We played soccer, football, together.

speaker: interviewer
text: He took a liking to my sister. He was forbidden to come to our house. God forbid he's going to marry a foreigner. And he deliberately said foreigner, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said the nasty, nasty sound of the word. So there was no friendship. There was no relationship between one and the other.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they were all as orthodox as our orthodox here, only no [NON-ENGLISH], no tzitzits hanging out, as-- as German-looking as anybody else. And my father disliked them as much-- they disliked us because we were not German born. But you know, what is a difference between a kid that was born in Vienna? They speak the same German language, with the exception of some words that the Viennese know that the Berliners don't. So after you finished the two years of the-- the Gymnasium, right? You came to the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So can you describe for me what it was like when you first got to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the first thing is looking up. So, so big. So big. This is the tremendous impression it makes, I suppose, on anybody, no matter what his or her background is, when they see the skyscrapers. It was absolutely fantastic. And-- [LAUGHS] I don't know whether I should tell that story. There's an uncle of mine, one of those who was shipped here when he was finished with Gymnasium, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and the one that became a general. He came to see me. He-- he got on the boat. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You know what he did, first thing, before he even asked, how is your mother-- meaning his sister-- before he even asked, how is your-- he pushed a button to bring the man in. I had a word for the servant of some kind. Forgotten. The porter. Rang for the porter, and he talked to him quietly. I didn't know what he was saying. And then he disappeared, and about five minutes later, he came back with a big pail with a scrub thing over it. And then he took my suitcase, opened it up, and didn't ask any questions. Took the drag off at the top, put a bottle here, put a bottle there, put a bottle. It was Prohibition time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was, Oh my God. They're going to find it. They're going to send me back. I was frightened. You finally had all of the luggage together. You know, you'll find it under the letter R. You know.

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- everything ready. He went to a guy, talked to him. He came. I know where the-- the bottles were put. He knew how to find four bottles.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we get off of the pier, to the hotel, and there are some more uncles, the youngest of the uncles, Willy. You think they ask how is my sister? How many bottles did Julius get? You know what? Julius took the next train back to Chicago-- the other family lived in New York-- as quickly as [LAUGHS] he could. We were in the 20th Century. My impressions of America.

speaker: interviewee
text: I never knew that Prohibition could make a whole nation so crooked. I got to know Al Capone when I was a newspaper man in Chicago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I think we'll get to that in a moment. When you came to the United States, you obviously brought with you, in your mind, what was beginning to happen in Germany. Am I correct in assuming that?

speaker: interviewee
text: What was beginning to--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was beginning to happen in Germany, with the-- with the-- with the National Socialists.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: Or did you take it very seriously at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no, no. I mean, I-- I told you about the swastika thing there. And there was sometimes a dirty word. [SPEAKING GERMAN]. Words like that. But not too much. The real impact that the Nazi business made was a little bit later. Now, I'm going to jump, and then, later on, you can go and jump back. I-- I pick up after skipping something. I wound up working in a factory in Chicago. And at night, I translated United Press and Associated Press cables and wires into German for a chain of German-language newspapers. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] In 1929, in August, 1929, was the 10th anniversary of the adoption of the Wiemar Constitution. The Constitution of the Republic-- German Republic. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This-- the publisher of this chain of newspapers had a bright idea. He had about 20 German-language newspapers. All of them the same, only a different masthead, and two columns local stuff. So in Bismarck, North Dakota, they had the-- I'm making this up, now-- You know, whatever. And the work another name. But in each of these cities, they had a guy writing two columns for the front page. The rest of them was-- so the bright idea was the International Advertising Convention was held in Berlin at the time the 10th anniversary of the adoption of the Wiemar Constitution was held.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he sends to me and another fellow to Berlin to put out a daily paper. A big paper, about 24 pages, big size. And the first day, and then, the rest of the week, tabloid size, and only in German and in English. To promote-- the name of the chain was National Weeklies. Because all of the big advertising agencies who are there, this is what he was after. So this is 1929. So the people who supported the Wiemar Republic-- let me describe them as Republicans, but it has nothing to do with the Republican Party versus Democrats. But a republic instead of kaiser, right? They had a tremendous parade of what was called the Reichsbanner-- Reich, S at the end, and then banner, like you write 'banner.' Black, red, gold, that was our-- the banner.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had a tremendous parade. And I saw this, marching down again there's-- under the linden place that I mentioned earlier. And I said to my father, gee, this is fantastic. Incidentally, you should know, these were the fellas that marched in uniforms, almost as-- looking almost the same as those of the Nazis. Only they had different insignia.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said to my father, I says, fantastic, huh? He says, wait until next Sunday, when you'll see the stormtroopers making a counter-parade. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is really what you should have seen. 1929.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I saw that, I said to my father, Papa, when Walter-- that's my kid brother-- when Walter finishes school, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] see that he learns to do and earn a livelihood with his hands. A carpenter, butcher, what have you, so when he comes to America-- taking it for granted he would come-- when he comes to America, he can earn a livelihood with what he can do. I just went in the factory without even knowing what to do there. And this is a part of my finger that I left behind. So I-- this is the impact that made on me when I saw that. The Hitler people are going to take over. This was 1929. And I was right, and my father was right. That was [INAUDIBLE] impact.

speaker: interviewer
text: This-- this parade that you-- the one you saw the week after-- was-- was Eric Rohmer, were the SR part of this or were--

speaker: interviewee
text: The SR, yeah. It was-- it was the SR. But I should say that the Reichsbanner, which our-- they were the supporters of the Republic, without any reference to Republican here. They were primarily there to protect meetings of the Republic, of the Republicans. Because, you know, there were fights going on, even in the Reichstag, the German House of Parliament, they would break off the-- the legs of a chair and hit the other guy. And of course the Social Democrats and the Socialists and the Communists, they did the fighting, too. I mean.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you then in Berlin for the coverage of the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Only about a week.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I came-- well, maybe I should mention that. The publisher of the was Colonel Frank Knox, who was a running mate of one of the guys who ran against Roosevelt. I don't know whether it was Wilkie, or which one of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was there, attending. And when he saw this paper and somehow discovered I had something to do with it, he said, well, I've-- I've got an expert. Here's a guy who comes from Berlin. Where do I go for entertainment? Cabaret.

speaker: interviewee
text: Between you and me and the lamp post, I didn't know. So my sisters, who were older than I, I said to them, got to tell him where to go, so-- So this is the first time I went into a cabaret in my life. We had a nice evening together. And I said something to him. I said, look, you have, on page 2 and 3, foreign correspondents who are just as able as the foreign correspondents of the If you would run more stories, short stories, about the hyphenates-- the German-Americans, the Greek-Americans, the Polish-Americans, inside of the pages, you could build your circulation. He said, bright idea. Come and see me when you get back to Chicago. I went to see him, and I had a job. But I'd smelled within a week that I was not liked by the other guys. Marxist spy watching over us. And I had an opportunity to go to work for the the flagship of the Hearst newspaper empire. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then, [INAUDIBLE], then we move in. Next, we move over to the Nazis, unless you have some other question.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. Let's move right on to the Nazis.

speaker: interviewee
text: Go on?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm. To the Nazis.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was still with the German-American newspapers, I discovered-- no, not-- not discovered. That's not the right word. I found out that the German-- some of the German-American organizations were already beginning to admire Hitler. And some of them had Hitler literature, and so on, and so forth. I [INAUDIBLE] Uncle Julius fought them within the-- let me describe it. [INAUDIBLE] Its name-- I described it as-- as a federation of German-American organizations. There were singing societies, and Turner-- Turner gymnasts-- Turner societies. There were [SPEAKING GERMAN]. If you came from Bavaria, there was a people from Bavaria they're getting together. Like in the early days here in Los Angeles, you had the Iowa Society and the Michigan Society. You know, it's the-- the-- something they'd brought with them from over there. So that's the influence began.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we-- we were fighting them. Uncle Julius and I were fighting them. In this national organization. The Chicago-- the World's Fair was held in Chicago in 1933.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hitler came to power on January 30th, 1933. And there was an Italian house, and there was a Russian-- no, not Russian. A French house, and they have stuff-- stuff on like, exhibition, hoping that people would order this machine from-- and so on, so forth. There was a German house. And the first big fight that we fought, Julius and I, was to keep them from putting a Nazi flag on top of the German house over there. We won that fight.

speaker: interviewer
text: The other thing about the Nazis at that time was, does a name of a book mean anything to you? Name of the book is So I don't need to describe it.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was an anti-war book. The name of the author was Erich Maria Remarque. His name was not Remarque. His name was really Kramer. He reversed it, so he changed his name to Remarque. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] A film was made of it, by Carl Laemmle, Jr., at Universal. He was only 21. No, no, he was about my age. 26-- No. He was in the early 20s.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Nazis succeeded in keeping that film from being shown in Chicago. Why? Because an anti-war picture while they were pro-war. They used mice, released them in the-- in the motion picture theaters.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you know-- you know how you-- a bunch of women react. Ooo! You know, like that. So then, they brought pressure on the police department for the police department to ban that-- that picture. That's when we lost our first-- first fight. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Comes January 30th, 1933. I think I mentioned it before. Hindenburg is bludgeoned into [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] appointing Hitler as Chancellor. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And Julius and I [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] stopped [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] just arguing in this federation. And he pulled out a newspaper. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Hundred-- no, '10,000 Pastors Against Nazis.' This was the first anti-Nazi newspaper printed in the United States. Aimed primarily-- aimed primarily at German-Americans in the middle west.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you and your uncle Julius put this newspaper--

speaker: interviewee
text: Uncle Julius raised the money, and I, in my free time-- did I get across that I went to work for Hearst before? Yeah, I did. Aimed primarily at the German-Americans in the middle west. And Wisconsin was 40% German-Americans. Iowa was full of German-Americans. In order to counter the propaganda that just flowed in like a big stream into all areas, but particularly the-- the middle west. And we kept publishing this until I came to Los Angeles. And then-- then the we gave it up.

speaker: interviewer
text: So while you were in Chicago, the extent of what you did was putting out the newspaper. Is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I worked regularly, full time, for the And then at night and weekends, I worked on this paper. Eight pages, once a week. Was hard work. Hard work.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get a lot of your information for that newspaper?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, some of it-- some of it I secured from people who left Germany and got into Czechoslovakia. And Czechoslovakia was still-- still free, was not sold down the river by Mr Chamberlain. Some of it, by just watching. Watching what was going on. But it was more of what the Nazis were doing than what the Nazis in Chicago were doing. I mean, it was more international coverage than anything else.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what sort of influence in Chicago did the Nazis-- or in the Chicago area. Do you remember the Nazis having-- do you-- do you remember anything about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they had their-- [LAUGHS] I discovered then-- see, I came in '27. I discovered, when we began fighting them, that the first Nazi organization was started in Chicago in 1924. That was, again, the same year Hitler was in a concentration camp. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And here, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] later on, I came into investigating. And I will say more about it later. Investigating the Nazis for Army intelligence. But I'm just jumping now, and we'll-- we can go back. Here is [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] part of a page of the minutes of the first Nazi organization. It went under the name of Teutonia. Like Teuton. T-E-U-T-O-N-I-A. The book of minutes, 19, showed when it was start-- their organization was started in 1924. And this particular part shows how they collected money to send to Hitler, to Landsberg, you know, where the-- where his prison was.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get these minutes?

speaker: interviewee
text: But this I secured later when we went in to the Nazi investigation. And-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] In here you see the first Nazi stormtroopers in Chicago.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have your hand on the picture. You have to take your hand away. OK. Good.

speaker: interviewee
text: Is that all right now?

speaker: interviewer
text: I think-- Is that OK? Raoul?

speaker: interviewee
text: And in the middle of it, you see a man by the name of Kappe-- K-A-P-P-E, who was brought back to Germany later and trained saboteurs to come to the United States. They were landed at the-- at the-- is it New Jersey coast. They were captured. But the leader of them, who trained them, was not among them. They were captured. Some of them were condemned to death, and some of them, if they're still alive, are still in jail. Now, the-- the whole thing is a history of the beginning of the Nazi movement in the United States. And here shows Fritz Kuhn-- oh, incidentally, I forgot to give you the first name of Kappe. His first name was Walter. Walter Kappe. Here you see Fritz Kuhn, the national leader of the Bund, handing money to Hitler after Hitler became Chancellor. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Somebody want to take this out of my hand.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This can go out of my way, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So here you are in Chicago.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you manage to come-- go from Chicago to Los Angeles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that-- that-- we have to stay in Chicago a little longer. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After Hitler came to power, we, Julius and I, he's my uncle, but I never called him uncle. Julius and I kept fighting the Nazis every way we could. One fine day-- and they knew we were fighting. We went face to face. We went to the-- to meetings and gave them trouble. Mostly we-- we organized people out of the labor unions to go with us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One fine day, one of the members of what you knew as the Bund-- well, maybe I should stay with the name for a moment. Remind me to talk about one member. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] From Teutonia, it became Friends of the Hitler Movement. Then it became Friends of the New Germany, after Hitler came to power. And then it became known, and this is the way most Americans know them, as the German American Bund. One fine day, one of the Nazis came to see me. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He had a fight with somebody. And he was going to come to our side.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was from him that I got that book of minutes, to which I referred earlier. And from him, I got a lot of information. And now Hitler's in power, right? One fine day, he brings me something that shows me what I believe to be attempted espionage. Spies.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know what they call a morgue in a newspaper. When you-- when a newspaper man writes a story, not necessarily for publication, but gives the facts that go into what they call the morgue. He tells me about it, and I write a memo for the morgue about it. And I said, attempted espionage, morgue.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two days later, I find in my little box, the boss wants to see you. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was still very green. The boss wants to see me. Why? What did I do wrong with it? I went to see him. You write this? Or no, first I should identify him. The publisher of the was General Roy D. Keehn. K-E-E-H-N. General of the Illinois National Guard. The commanding officer. You write this? Yes, sir. Can you give me about three or four pages about this outfit? Yes, sir. How soon can I have it? I don't know how I answered. At any rate, when I had it ready, I submitted it to my city editor, who then got it upstairs.

speaker: interviewee
text: Second call. Oh, I should say, my-- my thesis was six pages long. [LAUGHS] I want you to go and see this man. And he hands me a slip of paper, oh, not any bigger than this here. And on it, it says, Mr. Marshall, Bismarck Hotel. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] See him as soon as you can. I went over to the Bismarck Hotel, phoned from the lobby. Joe Roos, he was-- I understand you were expecting me. Yes. Come on up. Turns out Colonel George C. Marshall. Colonel George C. Marshall, whom you knew later as General George C. Marshall. The greatest man, in my opinion, of World War II. Greater than Churchill. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I come from a pacifist home. I became a member of the Illinois National Guard and conducted the first investigation of Nazi activities in this country, under the supervision of Colonel George C. Marshall. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They got me a couple of Americans who could only talk English, and they got me a couple of German-Americans who were good. And this was the first investigation, as I said.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of investigation did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were undercover people. They joined what you now think about as the German American Bund. And they-- I taught them how to report, what to look for, and so on, and so forth. And how did I know? Marshall told me. Marshall was there. This is off-- no, I better not say. Marshall was-- let me put it this way. Marshall was there for a good certain reason. I will not describe it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it that you told them to look for? Can you describe in detail for me what you told them to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Keep your eyes wide open. Never get into any arguments with anybody. Talk as if you are in agreement with them. Don't-- don't egg them on in order to have a better story to tell me. Taught them how to write. Those that knew how to use a typewriter-- that time your still had to-- without any electricity, you know, the typewriter. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And those who couldn't type, some girls in the examiner's office would type up with us. And then all of this went to Keehn, and Keehn ended, but I did not know what he did. But later on, I discovered it, and I don't think anything would be gained by my telling how this, all of a sudden-- the-- the information-- some of the information I secured all of a sudden appeared in a certain weekly magazine published in New York. I know how it got there, but I wouldn't talk about it. I don't think it was-- what was done was right. It should have been kept by the government.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. So you-- just for myself, to clarify this-- you feel that this was information that shouldn't have been made public yet.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: It should have been kept under lock and key.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. At that time, I was very much public-minded. I mean, I wanted the public. I was like this. A fellow who knows how to write a newspaper, I wanted to tell the whole world. As a matter of fact, when they gave me-- when I left Chicago to go to Los Angeles, they gave me a bound volume of all of the issues that I put out. And they wrote a very beautiful dedication in it, sort of complimenting me as one who has seen the dangers on-- on the horizon.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you continue this-- these investigations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think we began going hard the moment Hitler came to power. This is, again, January 1933. And I came to Los Angeles in 1934. And just before I left, those people who helped to raise the money, or were interested, 95% of them Jewish, sort of gave me a razzle-dazzle au revoir, and gave me that volume.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to stop here and change the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to stop and change the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's 30 minutes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Is it that time? Time--
